content.problemStatement:
Chef is playing with weights. He has an object weighing WWW units. He also has three weights each of X,Y,X, Y,X,Y, and ZZZ units respectively. Help him determine whether he can measure the **exact** weight of the object with one or more of these weights.

If it is possible to measure the weight of object with one or more of these weights, print `YES`, otherwise print `NO`.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of single line containing a four positive integers W,X,Y,W, X, Y,W,X,Y, and ZZZ.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line `YES` if it is possible to measure the weight of object with one or more of these weights, otherwise print `NO`.


You may print each character of the string in either uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `yes`, `YES`, `Yes`, and `yeS` will all be treated as identical).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains four space‑separated positive integers W X Y Z.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the weight W can be obtained by using one or more of the given three weights, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: No subset of {2,1,6} sums to 5.
Test case 2: Weight 7 equals the second weight.
Test case 3: 8+12=20 using first and third weights.
Test case 4: No combination of {10,11,12} gives 20.
First case: No subset equals 1. Second case: 2+2+2=6.

solution.approach: For each test case we only have three weights, so there are only 2^3‑1 = 7 non‑empty subsets. We compute the sum of each subset (X, Y, Z, X+Y, X+Z, Y+Z, X+Y+Z) and check whether any of them equals W. If a match is found we answer YES, otherwise NO. This runs in O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
